chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Python, a high-level interpreted programming language famous for its zen-like code.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It's arguably the most popular language in the world because it's easy to learn, yet practical for serious projects.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: In fact, you're watching this YouTube video in a Python web application right now.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It was created by Guido Van Rossum and released in 1991, who named it after Monty Python's Flying Circus,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: which is why you'll sometimes find spam and eggs instead of foo and bar in code samples.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It's commonly used to build server-side applications, like web apps with the Django framework,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: and is the language of choice for big data analysis and machine learning.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Many students choose Python to start learning to code because of its emphasis on readability as outlined by the zen of Python.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Beautiful is better than ugly, while explicit is better than implicit.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Python is very simple, but avoids the temptation to sprinkle in magic that causes ambiguity.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Its code is often organized into notebooks, where individual cells can be executed, then documented in the same place.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: We're currently at version 3 of the language, and you can get started by creating a file that ends in .py,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: or .ipynb to create an interactive notebook.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Create a variable by setting a name equal to a value.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It's strongly typed, which means values won't change in unexpected ways, but dynamic, so type annotations are not required.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: The syntax is highly efficient, allowing you to declare multiple variables on a single line,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: and define tuples, lists, and dictionaries with a literal syntax.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Semicolons are not required, and if you use them, an experienced Pythonista will say that your code is not Pythonic.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Instead of semicolons, Python uses indentation to terminate or determine the scope of a line of code.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Define a function with the def keyword, then indent the next line, usually by four spaces, to define the function body.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: We might then add a for loop to it, and indent that by another four spaces.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: This eliminates the need for curly braces and semicolons found in many other languages.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Python is a multi-paradigm language.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: We can apply functional programming patterns with things like anonymous functions using Lambda.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It also uses objects as an abstraction for data, allowing you to implement object-oriented patterns,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: with things like classes and inheritance.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: It also has a huge ecosystem of third-party libraries, such as deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: and wrappers for many high-performance low-level packages like Open Computer Vision,

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: which are most often installed with the pip package manager.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: This has been the Python programming language in 100 seconds.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Hit the like button if you want to see more short videos like this.

chunks.title: Python
chunks.text: Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.

text: Python, a high-level interpreted programming language famous for its zen-like code. It's arguably the most popular language in the world because it's easy to learn, yet practical for serious projects. In fact, you're watching this YouTube video in a Python web application right now. It was created by Guido Van Rossum and released in 1991, who named it after Monty Python's Flying Circus, which is why you'll sometimes find spam and eggs instead of foo and bar in code samples. It's commonly used to build server-side applications, like web apps with the Django framework, and is the language of choice for big data analysis and machine learning. Many students choose Python to start learning to code because of its emphasis on readability as outlined by the zen of Python. Beautiful is better than ugly, while explicit is better than implicit. Python is very simple, but avoids the temptation to sprinkle in magic that causes ambiguity. Its code is often organized into notebooks, where individual cells can be executed, then documented in the same place. We're currently at version 3 of the language, and you can get started by creating a file that ends in .py, or .ipynb to create an interactive notebook. Create a variable by setting a name equal to a value. It's strongly typed, which means values won't change in unexpected ways, but dynamic, so type annotations are not required. The syntax is highly efficient, allowing you to declare multiple variables on a single line, and define tuples, lists, and dictionaries with a literal syntax. Semicolons are not required, and if you use them, an experienced Pythonista will say that your code is not Pythonic. Instead of semicolons, Python uses indentation to terminate or determine the scope of a line of code. Define a function with the def keyword, then indent the next line, usually by four spaces, to define the function body. We might then add a for loop to it, and indent that by another four spaces. This eliminates the need for curly braces and semicolons found in many other languages. Python is a multi-paradigm language. We can apply functional programming patterns with things like anonymous functions using Lambda. It also uses objects as an abstraction for data, allowing you to implement object-oriented patterns, with things like classes and inheritance. It also has a huge ecosystem of third-party libraries, such as deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow, and wrappers for many high-performance low-level packages like Open Computer Vision, which are most often installed with the pip package manager. This has been the Python programming language in 100 seconds. Hit the like button if you want to see more short videos like this. Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.
